title: Ship a SaaS MVP in 30 Days
subtitle: A day-by-day build log, from idea to first paying customer
description: A hands-on masterclass that walks through building, shipping, and monetising a real SaaS product in 30 days — with code, deployment, and customer acquisition all covered.
targetAudience: Intermediate developers who want to ship a product, not just learn a framework
objectives: Validate an idea before writing any code
Ship a minimal working product in week 1
Add auth, payments, and a database by week 2
Instrument, deploy, and monitor in week 3
Launch publicly and close first customers in week 4
Know when to throw code away and rewrite
prerequisites: Working knowledge of a web framework
Comfortable with git and the command line

modules.title: Week 0: Before You Write Any Code
modules.description: The discovery work most builders skip.

lessons.title: Talk to Five People
lessons.description: The cheapest validation you'll ever do.
lessons.content: Before a single line of code, interview five people who have the problem you're solving. Ask what they currently do and what they've tried. If you can't find five, you haven't picked a real problem.
keyPoints: Ask about past behaviour, not future intent
Silence is data — not hearing back is a signal
Listen for the duct-tape workarounds people build

lessons.title: Landing Page First
lessons.description: Test demand with a page, not a product.
lessons.content: Build a one-page site describing the product. Put up a waitlist form. Run a small ad campaign or post in a relevant community. If nobody signs up, nobody wanted it.
keyPoints: Traffic without signups means the page doesn't explain the value
Signups without pain means you have a nice-to-have
Paid pre-orders beat free waitlists as a demand signal

lessons.title: Pick the Stack in 30 Minutes
lessons.description: Boring technology for the win.
lessons.content: Use what you already know. A familiar stack you can ship with beats a trendy stack you're learning. Next.js + Postgres + Stripe is a perfectly good answer for most SaaS MVPs.
keyPoints: Boring tech is a feature, not a limitation
Managed services (Supabase, Neon, Vercel) remove whole categories of work
You can always rewrite later if you have the traction problem

lessons.title: Scope the MVP Brutally
lessons.description: What can come out without breaking the product.
lessons.content: List everything you want to build. Sort by necessity. Ship the top third. The user needs one path through the product that works; everything else can wait until people pay for it.
keyPoints: If a feature's absence doesn't block anyone from paying, cut it
Perfectionism is a form of procrastination
The ugly version that ships beats the polished version that doesn't

lessons.title: Day 1: Scaffold and Deploy
lessons.description: Get a blank page live on day one.
lessons.content: Run create-next-app (or your framework equivalent), push to GitHub, connect to Vercel. By end of day one, a blank page on a real domain should exist. Nothing else.
keyPoints: Deploy before you build — the deploy is the hard part
Never have undeployable code
A working CI/CD pipeline on day one pays dividends for 30 days

lessons.title: Day 2: Database
lessons.description: Set up managed Postgres.
lessons.content: Provision a managed Postgres instance (Supabase or Neon). Write a tiny migration that creates one table. Read from it in a route. Deploy.
keyPoints: Managed Postgres saves you from database ops entirely
Start with one table; schemas can grow
Connection pooling matters from day one in serverless

lessons.title: Day 3: The Core Action
lessons.description: Build the single thing the product does.
lessons.content: Your product has one central action users take. Build it end-to-end with no auth, no fancy UI. Just: input → server → database → rendered output. Get this working for yourself.
keyPoints: Prototype without auth first — auth can be added late
Server actions or route handlers are fine; API routes can wait
Test by using it yourself for a day

lessons.title: Day 4-5: The Second Action
lessons.description: List, view, edit the thing you created.
lessons.content: Once creation works, add the list view and detail view. Now the user has a minimal loop: create, view, modify. That's often enough for the first week's demo.
keyPoints: Lists before editing — editing is a harder UX
Pagination can wait until you have more than 50 items
Sorting and filtering are v2 features

modules.title: Week 2: Auth and Payments
modules.description: Make the product account-based and chargeable.

lessons.title: Day 8: Auth with a Managed Service
lessons.description: Don't roll your own.
lessons.content: Use Supabase Auth, Auth.js, or Clerk. Email magic links or OAuth cover 90% of users. Skip password-based auth if you can — it's a decade of footguns.
keyPoints: Rolling your own auth is technical debt on day one
OAuth and magic links avoid the password question entirely
Email deliverability will bite you if you don't use a reputable provider

lessons.title: Day 9-10: Scope to User
lessons.description: Make data per-user, not global.
lessons.content: Add a user_id column to every table. Filter every query by the current user. Row-Level Security in Postgres can enforce this automatically if you're using Supabase.
keyPoints: RLS in Postgres turns authorisation into a database concern
Test cross-user access explicitly — that's where leaks hide
Admin views should go through a separate policy

lessons.title: Day 11: Stripe Checkout
lessons.description: The shortest path to a first dollar.
lessons.content: Stripe Checkout is a hosted page that handles the whole purchase flow. Redirect to it; listen for the webhook; flip a database flag when the payment succeeds. An afternoon's work.
keyPoints: Hosted Checkout avoids PCI scope entirely
Webhooks must be idempotent — retries will happen
Store the Stripe customer ID — you'll need it for subscriptions

lessons.title: Day 12-14: Subscription Logic
lessons.description: Recurring payments and gating.
lessons.content: Switch to a subscription price. Handle subscription.updated and customer.subscription.deleted webhooks. Gate premium features behind the subscription status stored in your database.
keyPoints: Read the Stripe webhook documentation carefully — there are many events
Subscription cancellations can be immediate or at period end
Stripe's customer portal saves you from building billing UI yourself

quiz.question: Why should Stripe webhook handlers be idempotent?
options: Stripe may retry delivery, causing duplicate events
Idempotency is a legal requirement for PCI compliance
It reduces the webhook signing latency
quiz.explanation: Stripe retries webhooks on non-2xx responses or timeouts, so a handler that processes the same event twice must not double-charge or double-grant access.

modules.title: Week 2.5: The Polish Pass
modules.description: Make it look like a real product.

lessons.title: Empty States
lessons.description: What the app shows when there's nothing yet.
lessons.content: Every list view needs an empty state that explains what to do next. A blank screen with no call to action is the most common SaaS first-impression disaster.
keyPoints: Empty state text is onboarding — write it carefully
Include a single primary action
Avoid the word 'nothing' in empty states

lessons.title: Error Handling
lessons.description: When things break gracefully.
lessons.content: Network errors, auth expiry, validation failures — each needs a user-visible path. Never leave a user staring at a loading spinner that never resolves.
keyPoints: Toast notifications for transient errors
Inline validation for form fields
Retry buttons for operations that can be retried safely

lessons.title: Loading States
lessons.description: Perceived performance matters.
lessons.content: Optimistic updates make the app feel instant. Skeleton screens beat spinners. Above all, don't block the whole page when only one piece is loading.
keyPoints: Optimistic updates require rollback logic
Skeleton screens preserve layout while loading
Prefetching on hover makes navigation feel instant

lessons.title: Mobile Once-Over
lessons.description: Your users are on phones.
lessons.content: Open the app on a phone. Fix the three worst things you see. Ship. Do it again next week. Don't try for perfect responsive design in the first 30 days.
keyPoints: Test on real devices, not just Chrome dev tools
The hamburger menu is usually the worst-scoped component
Touch targets must be at least 44 × 44 pixels

lessons.title: Error Tracking
lessons.description: Sentry in ten minutes.
lessons.content: Install the Sentry SDK. Every unhandled exception now goes to a dashboard with stack trace, user context, and release version. The ten minutes pays back within a week.
keyPoints: Server and client both need instrumentation
Release tagging links errors to deploys
User context helps reproduce reports

lessons.title: Structured Logs
lessons.description: Logs worth reading.
lessons.content: Write JSON logs from day one. Include request id, user id, and route. A log aggregator like Axiom or Logtail indexes them so you can query later.
keyPoints: Plain-text logs become useless fast at scale
Never log passwords, tokens, or PII
Request IDs let you trace a user through a multi-step flow

lessons.title: Metrics Dashboards
lessons.description: What to watch, what to ignore.
lessons.content: Four charts are enough for week 3: signups/day, active users, key-action count, and error rate. Every other dashboard is a distraction at this stage.
keyPoints: A handful of meaningful metrics beats a wall of them
Daily not minutely — you don't run an ops team yet
Relative trends matter more than absolute numbers

lessons.title: Alerting That Doesn't Page You for Nothing
lessons.description: Only wake up for things you'd fix right now.
lessons.content: Set up alerts only for: payments failing, signups dropping to zero, error rate spiking, or the site being down. Everything else can wait for morning.
keyPoints: Alert fatigue ruins whatever alert system you have
Every alert needs a documented response action
A pager that goes off for non-actionable noise will be ignored

modules.title: Week 3.5: The Private Beta
objectives: Fix their top issue before the next one

lessons.title: Onboard the First Ten Yourself
lessons.description: Concierge onboarding finds bugs you won't.
lessons.content: Schedule 20-minute calls with each of the first ten users. Walk them through signup. Watch what they get stuck on. Every pause they have is a UX gap you can fix that evening.
keyPoints: Screen-sharing reveals problems you can't see in analytics
Don't explain — watch them try on their own
Record the call (with permission) so you can review

lessons.title: Fix Forward Fast
lessons.description: Every call generates a bug list — burn it down.
lessons.content: After each call, ship fixes within a day. If user 2 hits the same issue user 1 hit, that's a process failure. The goal is zero repeat issues across the first ten.
keyPoints: Speed of iteration matters more than elegance of fix
Same-day fixes build user loyalty
Users who see their feedback turn into improvements become evangelists

lessons.title: Say No to Feature Requests
lessons.description: The customer is not always right.
lessons.content: Users will request features you shouldn't build. Listen for the underlying problem, not the proposed solution. Often the same pain underlies five different requests — fix that, not all five.
keyPoints: Ask 'what are you trying to do?' not 'would you use X?'
One customer's essential feature is another's bloat
Saying no is a core product skill

modules.title: Week 4: Launch
modules.description: Show up in public and close first customers.

lessons.title: Pick One Channel
lessons.description: Product Hunt, Hacker News, or your existing audience.
lessons.content: One channel, done well, beats four channels done half-assed. Pick where your actual users are and execute hard. For most devtools: HN. For productivity: Twitter + Product Hunt.
keyPoints: Know where your users already hang out
Don't launch on a Friday or in December
A warm list of early supporters matters more than any algorithm

lessons.title: Write the Launch Post
lessons.description: Why them, why now, why this.
lessons.content: The launch post answers: who is this for, what specific problem does it solve, and why does it exist. Avoid adjectives. Include a single demo video under 60 seconds.
keyPoints: The first sentence decides whether anyone reads the rest
Demo videos must start with the payoff, not the setup
Include pricing — hiding it signals you don't believe in it

lessons.title: Launch Day
lessons.description: What you actually do when the post goes up.
lessons.content: Be online. Respond to every comment within an hour. Share user stories as they come in. Do not check metrics every five minutes — set them to send you a summary every few hours.
keyPoints: Present attention beats scheduled attention
Respond to critics with curiosity, not defensiveness
Monitor the error dashboard — launch traffic breaks things

lessons.title: Day 30 Retrospective
lessons.description: What worked, what didn't, what's next.
lessons.content: On day 30, write it all down: what shipped, what metrics moved, what the first customers actually say. The document is the handoff from day-30-you to day-60-you. It will matter.
keyPoints: Write the retro while details are fresh
Honest metrics, not vanity ones
Next 30 days: one theme, not ten

quiz.question: Why is concierge onboarding for the first ten users high-leverage?
options: It automates the onboarding for everyone after
It reveals UX gaps that analytics can't surface
quiz.explanation: Direct observation surfaces friction points that quantitative analytics can't detect — users rarely report what they got confused by.

lessons.title: Kill or Commit
lessons.description: An honest 30-day checkpoint.
lessons.content: After 30 days you have enough data to know: are people actually paying? Are they actually using it? If the answer is no, that's valuable information — it's okay to stop.
keyPoints: Sunk cost isn't a reason to continue
A 30-day failed product teaches you more than most books
If you're going to continue, commit hard for the next 90 days

lessons.title: The First Quarter Plan
lessons.description: Pick one theme.
lessons.content: If you continue, pick one theme for the next 90 days — retention, or growth, or a specific customer segment. Trying all three at once produces nothing.
keyPoints: One theme — not three
Write it down; share it with any collaborator
Review weekly; revise monthly; don't change it mid-quarter

lessons.title: Hiring (Probably Not Yet)
lessons.description: Why the first hire is rarely the right move.
lessons.content: Revenue under $20k monthly almost always means you shouldn't hire. Contracting out specific bottlenecks (design, content) is smarter. Hiring too early destroys runway and forces you to be a manager before you're ready.
keyPoints: First hire should be for a clear, bounded, repeating task
A cofounder is a different decision — made months earlier
A contractor for 10 hours/week beats a fractional hire for most early needs

bonusResources.title: The Mom Test
bonusResources.description: Short, practical book on customer interview questions that don't bias toward false positives.

bonusResources.title: Stripe Atlas Guides
bonusResources.description: Free written guides covering the non-technical side of starting a SaaS.
